comments.title: Curses
comments.content: Does something like a multi-generation curse/hex exist? And if so is there a way to free a family from it?

replies.title: Re: Curses
replies.content:
Curses are badly misunderstood these days. I discussed them a while back in a post on the old blog. Here's the relevant passage:

"It occurs to me that some of my readers may not be familiar with the structure and function of curses. (What do they teach children these days?) The sort of thing you get in bad modern remakes of fairy tales, where someone inoffensive gets burdened with a dire fate that would not otherwise befall them, is strictly amateur stuff. Professionals know that the curses that matter are the kind that unfold by their own inexorable logic from the actions and attitudes of the accursed. The witch or wizard who finds it necessary or appropriate to pronounce a curse doesn’t have to make anything happen; he or she simply says aloud the unmentionable realities of the situation, states the necessary consequences, and leaves. The efforts of the accursed to avoid falling victim to the curse, without actually changing the things that make the curse inevitable, then proceed to drive the curse to its fulfillment."

That is to say, if there's a multigenerational curse on your family, it's because there's a multigenerational behavior pattern that creates and feeds and nurtures the curse. Identify what behavior patterns on the part of your family members are feeding the curse, figure out what benefits you get from those behavior patterns, and then give up those benefits, and you escape the curse. It's as simple as that. (Of course "simple" is not the same thing as "easy"...)

replies.title: Re: Curses
replies.content:
Ah, yes, I remember now...

“You whom Haggard holds in thrall
share his feast and share his fall.
You shall see your fortune flower
till the torrent takes the tower.
Yet none but one of Hagsgate town
may bring the castle swirling down.”

Thanks for the answer and the reminder.

comments.title: Getting Started
comments.content:
Hi JMG, a few questions if I may.

1. Choosing a pantheon. I was raised Christian and can trace ancestors who were active in the breakaway Free Church of Scotland in the 1840s. The other side of the family are Mediterranean Catholics. So I guess a Christian pantheon is in the blood but not something I feel particularly attracted to.

Alternatively I really like the idea of local spirituality and that maybe disembodied beings are connected to material geography. But ... although I was born here, my culture is not indigenous to this place. Reaching out to the local spirits feels a bit like spiritual tourism.

Do you have any tips for choosing a pantheon?

2. Divination. I have felt a strong push to develop a variation of divination that uses stones. I collected stones over a couple of years but didn't know how I would use them. Recently I came to the idea that I would use the idea of a virtue being the midpoint between two vices, and attribute a virtue and its pair of vices to each stone. I have a list of 13 virtues and their vices and 13 stones. I will ask for daily guidance by pulling a random stone from a bag and reflecting on that virtue and it's vices.

My question is, should a beginner be creating his own system for divination? Or am I better off using an established method? I do have some oracle cards which are really useful but more for long term guidance, not daily divination.

3. The SOP. I have a question about invoking and banishing the elements. To invoke I draw the symbol clockwise. I then complete the invoking by drawing the symbol anti-clockwise. Then to banish I draw the symbol anti-clockwise again ... is that correct? Or does the first anti-clockwise effort act as the banishing?

Many thanks for your time

replies.title: Re: Getting Started
replies.content:
1) Read the myths, choose a set of myths that speaks to you, and start praying to the deities in question. There isn't one perfect pantheon for anybody; you have your choice -- but it's a good idea to make the choice and then don't look back.

2) If you create a divination system from scratch as a beginner, it will embody all your personal imbalances and unresolved conflicts. Learn an established oracle first, get good at it, and then -- having been forced to face your personal difficulties by that experience -- you can craft one for yourself.

3) The first anticlockwise tracing is the banishing. You don't trace the symbol anticlockwise twice; you trace it clockwise while saying "I invoke..." and then anticlockwise while saying "And I banish..."

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

At some point in the (very entertaining!) diet-related discussions over the last few weeks you noted that a strict vegetarian diet may not be compatible with magical practice for some. While I of course entirely agree, interestingly enough, all the early modern Persian letter- or astral-magical manuals with which I'm familiar insist that such a diet must be kept for the duration of any operation, together with a strict regime of daytime fasting. (Other conditions include maintaining a state of ritual purity, etc.) And the operations in question vary in duration from a few weeks to a few years, the creation of a comprehensive prognosticon (jafr-i jami') taking the longest. Do you know of any Latin manual, or for that matter English, that similarly requires a vegetarian diet and/or fasting of the practitioner? What do you suppose the rationale is behind such a condition (not explicitly stated in my manuals)?

And thanks for the pointers last week -- following them up now. Yates, of course, I'm already familiar with, but had forgotten she ties it into the Reformation in that work. Speaking of forgetting, I've also just bought your Bruno translation, which does indeed look lipsmacking!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Quite a few of the old grimoires insist on fasting and austerities of various kinds for a certain period before and, of course, during a magical practice, along with such religious actions as attending Mass and confessing your sins. I've noticed that this is very common in Western systems of magic that don't involve daily ritual practice. Do the Persian systems you're familiar with have daily magical rituals to perform, or is it a matter of big important rituals at intervals, separated by periods of ordinary existence?

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
A few weeks ago there was a discussion of Marion Zimmer Bradley, her abusive history, and her reported experiences with the astral plane - you noted that "that’s way down in the lower astral. If that’s what somebody experiences when they make contact with the astral, they really, really need to clean up their act."

Could you please expand on the nature of the astral and its levels, and its relation to moral virtue?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's not a matter of moral virtue but of the degree to which consciousness has become reflective, and open to self-knowledge. (You can follow a strict rule-based morality to the letter and be no more conscious than a robot.) The lower astral is where the basic biological desires (food, sex, power, and so on) resonate most strongly, since it's closest to the etheric level, the level of life energies; the completely unreflective mind is jerked around by these drives like a puppet on its strings; and since human societies in the downward half of their life cycles inevitably end up full of manipulative gimmicks that exploit people by way of these drives -- advertising is the form that this takes in our civilization -- the lower astral becomes gunked up with reflections of these gimmicks and the drives they stimulate.

The middle astral is where you see the reflections of ordinary, semi-reflective human thought; the upper astral is where you see reflections of the timeless realities of the mental plane. You resonate with these by having an inner life that's more than just the automatic triggering of ordinary biological desires; the more reflective you become and the more conscious of your own inner life you become, the more you reflect the mental plane, and the higher you go. Again, this isn't the same as moral virtue, though moral virtue in the broad sense can be one of its side effects, for the simple reason that the more reflective you become, the less interest you'll have in the sort of crass wallowing in biological automatisms that is the antithesis of moral virtue.

comments.title: Hyperborea, Cool as!
comments.content:
Hi John Michael,

Thank you for providing your time and this forum for asking questions.

I have had a situation in the past year or so where I have provided someone with a much needed reality chat. I have known the person for many years and have a good rapport with them. The content of the reality chat was consistent with my own actions and life, and yet the person disregarded the discussion only to then come face to face with many unpleasant circumstances which could have been avoided.

I do not genuinely believe that any further action of magic and healing on my part would have made any difference. From what I could see, they had a very fixed story running in their heads and my words ran counter to their story. What interested me about that situation was that the person sought advice from a person that ‘told them what they wanted to hear’ – and they got stitched up (i.e. ripped off) in the process.

When I was a boss and supervised staff I used to allow people who were particularly resistant to messages to simply fail, so as to learn a positive lesson and perhaps shock them into making their minds slightly more receptive to new stories.

Given the future that we both agree upon, should I continue making attempts to assist some people with any free energy I have to chuck at such problems, or simply allow events to run their natural course, whilst sorting my own situation out? It is a complex problem and there is only so much free energy to chuck around.

Cheers

Chris

replies.title: Re: Hyperborea, Cool as!
replies.content: I tend to use a "rule of three" in such situations. I'll help anybody out of a self-generated problem once; I'll help them out a second time with a warning that if they land in the situation again, they're on their own; the third time, they obviously want to have that problem, so I leave them to enjoy it. Cold? You bet, but it allows me to ration time and energy for those who will actually benefit from help.

replies.title: Re: Hyperborea, Cool as!
replies.content:
Hi John Michael,

Thank you for both the reply and the new tool. I can tell that you have honed that particular sword! If it means anything, I always prefer to work with people one on one in such situations, but even then, sometimes there are limits to what can be done. Oh well.

Chris

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I've been working my way through your book Circles of Power, slowly committing the various "mapping" schemes at the beginning to memory. Anyway, one thing that's come up in my meditations is how to differentiate between the, as you put it, 'the principles, laws and habits of the level' and something that's being caused by something working it's way down/up from another level?

For example, how would a mage view something like cancer? Is it one of the habits of the physical level? Or is it coming from something elsewhere? And more generally, is there some way to figure out where something is coming from or does it matter? Maybe I'm being too literal with the metaphor...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Most phenomena are a combination of both. Cancer, for example, is usually a result of causes on the material plane (such as the toxic chemicals that saturate our environment) and subtler causes on the etheric plane. Thus it's generally most effective to respond to it on both levels at once, using both material and nonmaterial treatments simultaneously.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
A couple weeks back you commented that plastics had a deathly energy around them, due to being made up of corpses. Makes sense, but why then does wood have (to me) so much more of a positive energy to it? My thoughts on it are that wood is naturally circulatory, being made up of thousands of tubes. It's designed to carry nutrients up and down very efficiently, and also to carry telluric energy up to the leaves and solar energy down to the roots. So when you cut down a tree, the tree's spirit "drains" out of the wood fairly efficiently, and the careful attention and intention of a skilled craftsman - or of a family eating around the same table for decades - fills up those energetic channels instead. Is that anywhere near the right track?

The alternative is that plants just aren't as attached to their body parts as animals are, having a much less defined growth structure and being able to regrow from meristems and so on, which I feel might be somewhat true and somewhat wishful thinking on the part of someone who does like to craft with wood.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Most of a tree is actually dead; the living part of a tree is a thin layer right under the bark. Thus wood is a dead substance that's good at coexisting with living substances, and it keeps that property even when the living substance changes from cambium to you. Plastics are made, if I may be blunt, from fish that have been rotting for millions of years, and if you've ever been around a rotting fish, you know that they don't coexist with living things well!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
"Most of a tree is actually dead"

I find this suprising. I've been working with wood for over 25 years and i don't think I've ever thought of wood as "dead". There is a vibrancy in wood, that i wouldn't necessarily call alive, but certainly not dead. Would a stone in a stone wall be considered dead?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Dead in a biological sense, as in, not currently engaged in the complex biochemical processes that are the physical side of life. In that sense, yes, a stone is also dead -- it doesn't exactly have vital signs. (And if it's limestone, it once did.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Does that apply also to plastics made from cellulose or sugar?

replies.title: Uncontrollable urge to nitpick
replies.content:
1. Well, the parenchyma rays are still alive.
2. I think oil is mostly algae and plankton.
Sorry, can't help myself. - Dewey

comments.title: Ogham
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

In your books, the order of the first aicme is BLNVS. In works by all other authors I have come across, the order is BLVSN. In The Druidry Handbook, you briefly mention that the latter system may have evolved for some reason related to Christianity. Can you expand on this? And, can you explain why you choose to work with the former system?

How might I interpret the third, fourth and fifth fews when they come up in divination? Must I use your system by itself, rather than integrating several sources?

Many thanks,

Chris

replies.title: Re: Ogham
replies.content: The old term for the Ogham alphabet is "Beith-Luis-Nion," just as the word "alphabet" comes from alpha and beta -- in both cases it preserves the order of the letters. (Think of the way children refer to the English alphabet as "the A B Cs.") The phrase "Beith-Luis-Fearn" for the Ogham alphabet never appears in old sources. Thus BLN is almost certainly the original order of the Ogham fews (letters). BLNVS spells BeLeNVS, the name of the old Celtic sun god; the traditional lore I learned in Druid Revival circles claims that this was intentional, and the Christians jumbled it around to remove the name of a rival god from the Ogham. Since I'm not a Christian, and learned the Ogham in the Revival tradition, I did things the BLNVS way. You are of course free to do as you wish!

replies.title: Re: Ogham
replies.content:
On the other hand, and of course a person is free to reject this as well, all of the early* ogham stones are best transliterated to present "Ogham Irish" or "Primitive Irish" words - or, usually, names - by using the "BLVSN" order, and the earliest Irish text describing the system in detail also uses that order. The "BLNVS" order was developed speculatively based on the "Beth-Luis-Nion" term, assuming that it referred to the first three fedha, rather than the first, second, and final (fifth) fedha of the first group.

It's also probably worth noting in passing that the period when Belenus was associated with Apollo, as Apollo Belenus, was a period in which Apollo was not usually associated with the Sun**, but instead with His more normal attributes of medicine and herbalism, poetry, spears/arrows, and so forth. There's an extensive discussion of this cluster of symbols, though sadly missing any of the Celtic evidence directly, in Apollo the Wolf-God by Daniel Gershenson, which I highly recommend to any interested parties. Related to this, and helping to connect it with the Celtic evidence, is Lady With a Mead Cup by Michael Enright.

*I say "early", but I believe that this is true of all of the extant ogham stones that were set up before the seventeenth century and have been translated to date.

**Which is not to say that a person today can't associate the Sun with Apollo, obviously, only that when Apollo was originally associated with Belenus that was not a factor that mattered to the people making that association.

replies.title: Re: Ogham
replies.content: Of course! I find, though, that the BLNVS order works better for me in magic and divination than the historically attested BLVSN order, and so that's the order I use and teach.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: I'm approaching completion of the curriculum of Learning Ritual Magic and would like to continue the process of internal growth and change in the Golden Dawn tradition.   What published  source(s) of guidance would you recommend as the next step for the solitary learner.  My preference is to avoid joining a group of studying by correspondence.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Excellent! Congratulations for persevering in the work; most people who start a course of magical study and training never get to the point you've reached. Your next steps may be found in my books Paths of Wisdom and Circles of Power -- Learning Ritual Magic is intended as an introduction to the work in these two books. To these I would encourage you to add two books by Israel Regardie, Ceremonial Magic and The Golden Dawn; the developments of the Watchtower ritual in the first, if practiced systematically, will enable you to self-initiate into the full Golden Dawn egregor without messing with a temple or a correspondence school, and the second is of course a (nearly) complete collection of the original Golden Dawn papers and rituals, from which you can draw many years of increasingly creative, powerful, and effective magical workings. Those four books, plus systematic daily practice (the one non-negotiable requirement, as you already know), will take you to the summit of the Golden Dawn system. Again, congratulations; the Path stands open before your feet.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If one has completed a different course of Golden Dawn-related study, such as that found in Kraig's Modern Magick or the Ciceros' Self-Initiation into the Golden Dawn, would one then be able to make use of the 4 book curriculum you described? Or is it built to follow on Learning Ritual Magic specifically?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's specifically set up to work with Learning Ritual Magic, but you can apply it to other versions of the Golden Dawn curriculum. The one place you'll have to scramble a bit to get up to speed is meditation; I've found that the specific form of meditation taught in Learning Ritual Magic (and also covered in Paths of Wisdom and many of my other books) is a crucial "missing ingredient" in most versions of GD practice, and you'll need to learn that and make it a daily practice as soon as possible to get the most out of your work. (The basic daily practice in my version of the GD system is the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram and Middle Pillar exercise followed by 15-20 minutes of meditation; meditating in a space you've cleared by banishing, and in the state of balance you get from the Middle Pillar exercise, is extremely valuable. You then do a divination every day at some other time.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Should one not start out directly with Regardie's GD?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's the hard way to do it. The reason Kraig, the Ciceros, and I wrote more basic books is that the material can be presented more easily to the complete beginner.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you, POW has been on my radar for some time now so this strengthens my resolve to head there next.

replies.title: gd books
replies.content:
Hi.

I finished LRM a couple years ago and am now working through C of P and P of W. Let me know if you'd like to compare notes.

Jacques

williammcgillis@gmail.com

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
A technical question.

In the Analysis of the Keyword ritual, you have the Divine Light descend as a column of white light, filling the entire room. Other others (thinking particularly Don Kraig) have instead an orb of golden light, descending to the heart of the operator.

I've tried it both ways, and found that the light-column has less of an effect on my energetic body but much more efficacy as a general-purose blessing. Do you know where the different versions come from, and is it possible to do one or the other based on need, or do you favor only doing the column of lihjt method, or picking either one but sticking with it?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'd encourage you to experiment and do whichever gets the results that you favor. I wasn't familiar with the golden-orb version; I got the column of light version from an assortment of Golden Dawn papers plus my own experiences.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
And my mind is now blown. I just looked up the description of the Analysis of the Key Word in Kraig's Modern Magick, which I first read more than 5 years ago, and have practiced semi-regularly since...

You'll already have guessed that I found nothing at all about a golden orb.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That sort of thing happens all the time. The mind actually reconstructs its memories every time it remembers something, you know...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I seem to recall a golden orb in Kraig's first meditation exercise. Maybe you got it confused with that?

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: What are your thoughts on the afterlife/afterlives?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You can find a fairly detailed discussion of my views here.

comments.title: Travelling on the Astral plane
comments.content:
Hello!

Can we have a very brief description of your astral projection journeys and the techniques you use?

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Travelling on the Astral plane
replies.content: I tend to shy away from discussing my own spiritual experiences, because people very often respond in unproductive ways to that. Instead of worrying about my experiences, why not go have your own?

replies.title: Re: Travelling on the Astral plane
replies.content:
I have begun trying astral projections but to no avail. I was wondering if you could summarise the technics of astral projections so that I can check if I got something wrong.

Thanks

replies.title: Re: Travelling on the Astral plane
replies.content: It takes many people a long time to achieve it, and there are many different methods. You probably just haven't kept at it long enough. The instructions I used were those included in W.E. Butler's book Apprenticed to Magic, which you can certainly consult if you're curious. (He mentions one student who took seven years of regular practice to manage the trick, by the way...)

replies.title: Re: Travelling on the Astral plane
replies.content: I just got a copy of Melita Denning and Osborne Phillips' book on this subject. It is one of those things I suspect I wouldn't be good at, but the book was cheap and unusual, so I took the chance to bag it.  Do you have any strong opinion for or against that work?  Thanks!  - Dewey

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Last week there was a minor discussion on the subject of "Soul Death" - and you mentioned that further research(or simply speaking with a shaman) would have to be done to determine the equivalent term in western occultism.  Would you happen to have either a) given the matter more thought since then, or b) know of any avenue of such research(books, etc) that could be pursued by a layman on this subject?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I've been up to my eyeballs in writing a novel since then, and my research time has gone in other directions, so no, I haven't gotten into that. It's "soul loss" rather than "soul death," by the way. As for avenues of research, good scholarly works on shamanism -- by which I emphatically do not mean the commercial products of the late Michael Harner! -- would be a good starting place.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you, for both the correction(I realized my mistake re:loss vs death as soon as I'd sent the comment), and the direction.  I will make my own inquiries and avoid Michael Harner.

comments.title: Mixing Pantheons
comments.content:
John Michael,

First I want to thank you for the recommendation last week of Liz Greene's book on Saturn. Very informative and provocative and likely to produce another question or two in the future.

My question today involves whether it is advisable to mix different pantheons of gods in one's personal relationship with and worship of them. I am a Buddhist practicing in the Tibetan Nyingma and Kagyu lineages and have developed a serious devotion to the Medicine Buddha since receiving the empowerment to do his practice three years ago. (My livelihood has been in the healing arts for over 30 years and receiving this transmission was stunningly profound, life-changing for me.)

At the same time I feel close to some of the Greek pantheon gods and goddesses. And I have also been trying to open to local nature spirits with whom I share this land.

Is this mixing a problem? How would this background coordinate with the Sphere of Protection or other practices taught in the Druid Magic Handbook?

Thank you for your thoughts on this matter,
Yanocoches in Colorado

replies.title: Re: Mixing Pantheons
replies.content: If you're practicing in a specific tradition, you need to ask such questions of people who are competent experts in that tradition. I have less than no idea how the various contacts and energy structures of the Nyingma and Kagyu lineages would respond to such things! If you were a Druid, a Golden Dawn initiate, or a Western occultist generally, that would be a different matter, but it's emphatically true that different systems respond differently.  I'd encourage you to find an open-minded lama and talk to him or her about it.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Will a regimen of daily banishing, meditation and divination sustained over years lead to a yesodic level of consciousness or to something else entirely different?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That depends very much on what you mean by "a yesodic level of consciousness." From my perspective, you're actually conscious on the Yesod level already, and indeed on the Hod and Netzach levels as well -- that is to say, you experience the life force (in the form of "vibes," gut-level hunches, and suchlike feelings), thoughts, and emotions, as well as the sensory perceptions of Malkuth. The point of magical training is to take you beyond those ordinary human perceptions, first through the Veil to the functions of the higher self (the levels corresponding to Tiphareth, Geburah, and Chesed), and then across the Abyss to the roots of consciousness in the Supernals. Why stop at Yesod?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That certainly is the ultimate goal over many lifetimes.  I should have written something like "fully develop the potentialities of astral psychism in a balanced form", as represented by Yesod, which seems attainable in this lifetime.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: No, you want a different set of exercises if that's your goal. The training for seership given in Rudolf Steiner's books, especially How To Know Higher Worlds, is better suited for that more specific focus. The Golden Dawn training is oriented toward a balanced general awakening of the higher potentials of the self, of which astral psychism is only one, and far from the most important.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
John--

In my continuing meditation on the Tree of Life and the druidic elements, I found myself contemplating the schema of the four traditional elements (Air, Earth, Water, Fire) and the associated qualities (cold, dry, hot, moist). Is there any tradition of a similar parallel triad of qualities associated with the druidic elements? I've developed one possible schema, but I'd be curious as to how it squares (ha!) with any established tradition.

Also, I need to come up with another term for the Tree, as my re-imagining of its form in the course of these meditations has resulted in a completely different structure, while keeping the nodes and pathways intact. (It is something like a tetrahedron on stilts, with Malkuth at the apex and the three pillars beneath each of the three basal points.) More meditation on this!

I rather feel like I've wandered off the trail on this, but my meditations keep showing linkages and relationships and patterns, so I guess I'll keep following it. I'm taking notes, so perhaps I could write this up one day into a coherent system.

--David, by the lake

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I don't know of any such pattern with the Druid elements of calas, gwyar, and nwyfre; it would be useful to develop one. As for three-dimensional Trees of Life, that's a fairly common experience -- I've seen several versions of it, most of which do in fact involve tetrahedra and the like. By all means keep working on it, but don't neglect the traditional symbolism!

comments.title: Ancestors
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Any idea on how it is that an ancestor from centuries ago would be interested in locating, and then be able to contact, one or more members of their currently incarnated family? The word that occurs to me is “resonance.” Am I on the right track?

As always, I much appreciate your insights.

OtterGirl

replies.title: Re: Ancestors
replies.content: Beings outside incarnation have access to senses and perceptions we don't, and so can perceive things easily that we can't perceive at all. Resonance may be one of the factors involved. As far as his interest, he may plan on reincarnating in his old family -- that happens tolerably often.

replies.title: Re: Ancestors
replies.content: Interesting!  I’ll be 60 next year, so no births in the offing here.  The presences, when I am fortunate enough to experience them, are supportive.  I often wonder if they are simply further down the path, spiritually, than I am, and by their presence offer encouragement to persevere.

replies.title: Re: Ancestors
replies.content: Also a possibility. Have you considered asking them?

replies.title: Re: Ancestors
replies.content:
Yes, definitely. Have done so occasionally. What I get back is more feeling than words: An embracing sense of nurturing from my ancestral grandmother, and a sense of gentle but firm discipline from my ancestral grandfather. He appeared to me once in a vision just as I was waking up. A few seconds only, but long enough to make eye contact. It was a gift, for sure. They were of the same generation, but did not know each other in life. They were my late father’s great grandparents.

Typing this, it occurs to me they’ve given eloquent answers to my questions; just not in words.

comments.title: Question
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I had a strange incident and I'm wondering if you could shed some light on it. I was raised monotheist and have read the WoG, your blogs, and read some of your Celtic Golden Dawn book. So, neophyte in this realm.

I tried something that I read in one of the comment sections of these posts recently. When looking for a deity to say something like “I hope and believe that a deity would like to connect with me...” (Yikes, I've searched the last Magic Monday comments to find this exact phrase but can't locate it now.)

I have two greco roman deities that I feel comfortable with but was looking to see if there were any other deities near me or that might be attuned to household issues (the greek and roman pantheons not being known for happy families).

I did this ask and immediately felt a warmth on my shoulder. An entity appeared and took the form of my astrological sun sign animal so it immediately felt comfortable. I assumed this was good.

Another entity also immediately came to my front door and waited for permission to enter. It was a timid little thing. I invited it in. So these two were there and I asked “What sort of offerings would you like?” Immediately the animal shaped one grew fangs and was ravenously hungry, dangerously so. I used a new age protection technique I picked up many years ago (a rose) and pushed the entity out into the street. It was fighting, more than I thought I could manage, so I called on my greco-roman deities for help. For lack of better words, they seemed to beat this creature to a pulp in the street and it disintegrated. Gone. Completely.

The timid little house deity/creature has stayed and is very pleasant. When I asked what it wanted, it just said “a home” and felt like a stray kitten. Since then, this deity has gone around sweeping up negative energy and replacing it with light/good energy.

I suppose my first question is: what the heck happened? As a raised monotheist, this was quite alarming and I almost think I was dreaming. It was the middle of the night.

Second, I feel like I have made a lot of mistakes here. I have leafed through your CGD book but not begun in earnest, since I have a little one in the house and after reading some previous comments about not doing work with kids around, I have not pursued. Shortly before this incident, I did think I should start doing the SoP, as that might be safe and was going to implement that as daily practice but jumped ahead with this. Which seems to be one of my mistakes….

Any insight you could give would be appreciated, I'm as baffled and as I am ruffled. Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Question
replies.content:
This is why it's a really good idea to practice some kind of basic banishing ritual on a daily basis. There are a lot of nonphysical critters out there, and by no means all of them are safe to be around. The mere fact that something lacks a body doesn't make it wise or good! If you've got a good relationship with the Greco-Roman pantheon, stick with that -- Vesta might be a good deity to invoke for household purposes, and regular offerings to the Lares and Penates are always a source of good fortune for the household. Your small stray-kitten spirit could turn out to be a very powerful protector in due time, but we'll see.

I'd encourage you to begin the Sphere of Protection as a regular practice -- it's safe to do in a house where there are small children -- and be sure to include the names of your Greco-Roman deities among those you invoke in it. They'll appreciate it, and be more likely to protect you further.

replies.title: Re: Question
replies.content:
Hi anon

Intrigued by your experience.

When you say you saw the deities how did that work? Were your eyes closed or open? Were they seen plainly or was it vague?

comments.title: Focus and Energy for Writing
comments.content:
Dear John Michael Greer,

I remain in awe of your energy and focus as a writer-- and now your generosity in hosting both this blog and your other blog, Ecosophia. (Looking forward to reading your introduction to and translation of Bruno's ON THE SHADOWS OF THE IDEAS, by the way.)

My question is, have you used (and would you recommend) any specific ritual for improving energy and focus for writing?

Millicently Lurking

replies.title: Re: Focus and Energy for Writing
replies.content:
Not as such. The discipline I practiced for some time, to get rid of the habit of divided consciousness called "writer's block," was to sit down every day for fifteen minutes (I would set a timer) with a pen and a notebook, and write without stopping for that entire period. I mean "without stopping" quite literally -- the pen did not stop moving. Even if all I could think of at first was "I have no idea what I'm going to write," I wrote that, and kept writing about the fact that I had no idea what I was going to write, and how frustrated I was about the fact that I didn't know what to write, and what I wanted to accomplish by using the exercise, and so on -- and before long I'd filled several pages and fifteen minutes of time.

Writing with energy and focus is a habit, and like any other habit, you pick it up by doing it. Oh, and if you still have a television in the house, get rid of it. Those things eat the free time and energy you could otherwise use to write with!

replies.title: Re: Focus and Energy for Writing
replies.content:
Dear John Michael Greer,

Thank you for taking the time to answer my question.

In the past, I practised what I call "Morning Pages," as recommended by Julia Cameron, which sounds very much like what you are doing-- to start the day, just keeping the pen on the page and writing, no matter what. It helped me then... and your reply inspires me to resume that practice.

In my experience, and as you have said elsewhere, most people have plenty of time for writing (or whatever else their heart desires to do) if they would simply eliminate television. My TV went out the front door, never to return, some years ago. As for Netflix and the like, not all certainly, but ninety-nine percent of what's on there is poisonous distraction and so I do not watch it. No FB, nor Instagram, nor Whatsapp etc, either.

But again, I say, I am in awe of your writerly energy and focus.

With appreciation,

Millicently Lurking

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Going back as long as I remember I've had strange dreams of a particular variety. These dreams always occur when I am in the liminal state between wake and sleep, and in them I am always someone else. Not someone in a different situation from my real one, but a person with a complete set of memories. The memories are especially convincing because I am aware of them in the same way that you are currently aware of all the past events of your life-- like they're not at the center of your consciousness, but you could immediately call them to mind, and they're central to your awareness of yourself AS yourself.
Invariably, as soon as I try to catch the dream it ends, and I immediately forget first the circumstances of the Dream and then the identity. I have had at least hundreds, maybe thousands of such dreams over the course of my life and I am unable to remember a single concrete detail. All I remember is that they're usually mundane-- I am a different person in an otherwise normal life. Occasionally they have been stranger or more exotic, though again, nothing remains.

Is it possible these are past life memories?

I also occasionally-- more often when I was younger than now-- hear at the edge of my hearing voices and conversations, again onl ever in the state between sleep and waking. These are rarely clear enough to catch words-- It's like hearing a conversation in another room. Do you have any thoughts as to what is going on here?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Might be past life memories, might be simple telepathy. Regular meditation would likely develop the capacities of consciousness that would enable you to retain consciousness of the experience.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I am intrigued by your forthcoming analysis of the Aries ingress. I have made my own rudimentary analysis and shall be interested to see where we agree, disagree and what points didn't occur to me at all. In any case, as you said, it's a doozy.

However, my question is about astrology and the second world war. In an attempt to hone my own abilities in mundane astrology, I looked up the Aries ingress charts for London, Berlin and Warsaw in 1939 and couldn't see anything to suggest that a major conflict was about to occur.

Assuming that this was down to my own lack of experience, I tried searching for the official astrological interpretation of those charts. I found out that this was apparently a serious failure by the astrologers of the time to spot the impending war and that this had done serious damage to the credibility of astrology.

What's your take on this, does this failure undermine the whole art of mundane astrology?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Er, how about a Sun-Mars square within seven minutes of arc, with Mars exalted in Capricorn, Sun in Aries and thus ruled by Mars, Mars also square Moon and the Moon's ruler by position (Moon in Aries), Saturn and Mars in mutual reception (Saturn in Aries, Mars in Capricorn) putting Martial energies in the angular 10th house and thus massively strengthening them, and of course Saturn in the 10th, which is always a sign of impending catastrophe? Not to mention Pluto (which was near the peak of its strength at that time) conjunct the ascendant in the London and Paris charts...It's a ghastly ingress and, to my eye, screams trouble on the way.

The "official astrological interpretation" you're referencing was the Harrogate Conference of 1939, in which a bunch of respected astrologers (including Charles Carter) made fools of themselves by insisting despite all these factors that the heavens predicted peace. You need to understand the political climate of that time; a great many people in the British upper class in 1939 strongly sympathized with Hitler and backed the policy of appeasement, and the astrologers in question shared that background. (Astrology in Britain before the 1960s was largely a Tory habit.) It takes a certain amount of hard work to set aside your preferences and see a chart for what it is, and the Harrogate astrologers failed that test. People have been quoting them ever since as evidence that astrology doesn't work, when all it shows is that astrologers don't always work...

comments.title: Desires activated by the Watcher
comments.content:
Hello John.

I think I have just encountered the Watcher. I spent almost a year trying to answer the divergent question of ‘what magico-spiritual exercises do I want to practice’, and I found my answer. Almost immediately thereafter, my interest in spirituality became weaker and many other interests of mine became significantly stronger. For example, I haven’t been particularly interested in movies for years, and guess what I spent the last few nights doing.

I remember you saying that the solution to magical practices becoming less appealing was to Just. Keep. Doing. Them. But what about the other part of the equation, these newly-activated desires like movies, video games, etc.? Would you recommend indulging them in moderation, as long as I keep doing my practices? Or would it be better to refuse giving them any attention at all?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Desires activated by the Watcher
replies.content: It's very simple. Discipline yourselt to indulge in those other activities after you do your magical training. When you want to watch a movie, tell yourself, "Sure, just as soon as I do my daily practice." Then go do the daily practice, and reward yourself with the movie afterwards. You may find that your interest in the movie vanishes once it no longer functions as a distraction from your practice...

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi John,
I have been working out of your Druid Magic Handbook and the Druidry Handbook for 6 months now. And I have been doing the Elemental Cross and the SOP everyday since the beginning. Also, I have recently constructed a stone circle with a stone alter and a stone seat, both dolmen style.
I would like to relate two incidents that happened to me recently. The first incident took place while I was meditating in my standing stone grove. After a few minutes to quite myself, I began to color breath using white light. After a few moments of color breathing, a very bright entity appeared at the Lughnasadh position in the circle. This entity rapidly moved to the center of the circle on my right. This entity was a very bright purple with a very bright white aura where a head would/should have been, actually there was no discernible face or head. Needless to say I was very startled. I have to admit I panicked a little and told the entity to leave, which it did.
After the entity departed and I had time to regain my bearings, I through that maybe I should have engaged the entity, but my gut feeling was that I did the right thing.
The second incident occurred while I was performing a grove working. I was starting to call and banish the 4 elements, and while calling the Air, the yellow light of air moved out of the Air symbol in front of me and into me. The light was not just around me, but within me, I was the light. I remained this way for a few moments, I'm not sure how long. This also startled me, perhaps I too easily startled, but I continued on with the working, completing it, and low and behold we are now receiving abundant and plentiful moisture and precipitation in the area after having no measurable moisture since last Oct. So all's well that ends well. I would appreciate your thoughts on these two events. Thank you very much.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: These are very common experiences and indicate that you're starting to get definite results. They also suggest that you may have a talent for visual clairvoyance, which could become quite useful. Continuing your practices is the best way to continue to ripen that.

comments.title: mixing systems
comments.content:
I know that you recommend against trying to learn two different systems at the same time. This seems sensible. At one point while trying to learn Latin I had the uncomfortable feeling that the ablative might take root at the expense of my native English.

But what about continuing to use elements of an already mastered system while learning a new one? I have some magical techniques I have picked up through years of Wiccan training that I am comfortable with for specific uses: a minor healing, a protection, house blessings, etc. Is there any problem with continuing such practices while learning a different system such as the Celtic Golden Dawn?

Rita

replies.title: Re: mixing systems
replies.content: I hope that's not a problem, as I do it all the time. (Not with Wiccan practices -- I've never done Wicca -- but I've routinely maintained as many as two or three systems with occasional practices while learning something new.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: I have a question about the Razor's Edge, actually.  In it, Larry Darrell causes several people, including the narrator, to raise an arm above their heads merely by saying that they will, and without first hypnotizing them in any apparent fashion.  Is this just a novelist telescoping a more complicated phenomenon for dramatic purposes, or is that kind of suggestion something you've observed?  And if the latter, what is going on?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I've never seen anything like it, so I suspect Maugham was playing fast and loose with things for dramatic effect. Still, I don't know.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I suspect I am being gullible.  I badly want to believe the narrator's claim that the story is a lightly fictionalized account of actual persons and events.  The little research I did suggested that there was a real life Elliot Templeton, an anglicized American called Chips Channon.  But as for Larry Darrell, I suppose he is just a very attractive fiction.

comments.title: Past life memories
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I thought I recalled someone asking about past life memories before, and you said the best way for them to come up was through discursive meditation.

What would be the best way to meditate, and retrieve past life memories?

Would you do this by using your past life as a theme for meditation,and seeing what memories pop up, etc? Or perhaps by thinking about the earliest things you can remember and working back?

I know this may not have much spiritual benefit at first, and that there's room for self delusion if one isn't careful, its just something that's been popping up lately and I've become super interested in.

replies.title: Re: Past life memories
replies.content:
I like this question! Some additional questions to go off of this:

I'm curious as to when you meditate, are you supposed to clear out your mind for things to come to you or do you consciously pick a theme, concept, image to focus on and base the meditation on?

replies.title: Re: Past life memories
replies.content: You'll find complete instructions on the method of meditation I use in most of my books on occultism, including Learning Ritual Magic and The Druidry Handbook. Yes, it's the same method in all my books.

replies.title: Re: Past life memories
replies.content: Meditation isn't primarily a means of retrieving past life memories, and if you go into it with that purpose, your imagination will happily cook up a nice steaming pot of nonsense for you. Regular meditation on themes drawn from a spiritual tradition that appeals to you will develop, over a period of some years, the capacities of awareness that will let you start to perceive past life memories when you're ready for them. (You don't get to decide when you're ready for them, in case you're wondering. Your higher self does that.)

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
My Saturn returned in January, meanwhile I am transitioning into being the breadwinner for my parents, leading my first market garden, and dealing with the worst a Moon opposite Saturn can throw. I feel like the last three years leaning in have cast me in many different roles, like previews for the different men I could become. Are the next three years where I, to the degree I have developed a capacity for freedom, pick which to become?

During those three years I have been an on again off again aspirant for the Ovate grade. I have been following some conversation here on magic mondays about the 'watcher' and dang that there watcher has been pushing hard against me on meditating. See, I can do discursive meditation, pretty well if I am pacing, and have done so since I was a little kid. Complete with following stray thoughts back to the fork, and shifting forks only when I reach the end of a line, all that. But for the purpose of CGD I think it important develop some capacity at the sitting style; and it is shockingly hard to shift; frustrating like explaining a complicated thing in a language you barely know, when you have the option to just put it in simple english with no exertion. When I meditate as soon as I get to a real chewy thought I get this urge to pace and just let that thought be thunk. At which point the Watcher doubles down on me and, well I manage to do something of a meditation, but compared to what a good walk might produce its nothing I am proud of. Generally soon afterwards I go for a walk and get the idea right. It feels something like the way you might imagine a dance competition. Several times, round about meditation 4 or 5, I have gone chicken, frustrated, or gotten so ashamed of my half baked meditations that I have shifted to the most minimal practice and put study on the back burner for later. Then I would get serious again, but feel compelled to go back to the start, so those first three sections have been worked over like Horace Fletcher's oats, in contrast to a rough understanding of the latter sections. I feel like I am making progress, though slowly, over the last two months of work in the system I have done more than all preceding goes put together. Most importantly I have been given glimpses of how much of what I think or say about myself is malarkey. If I keep pushing on the limits the Watcher is placing before me, and pace myself is there a good chance that I can push though this wall to a second wind; if so any experience on the variety of durations such a phase my take to get through?

Final topic for this post. About a week ago, on a day where meditation was proving particularly challenging, it occurred to me that in my pacing meditations I would imagine each as addressed to a particular listener, the same thought need be dressed in different words, depending on the fashion sense of different ears. I also decided that for the purpose of my sitting meditations such a thing might be desirable, but that a living person wouldn't be a good imagined audience for that purpose. So on a flight of whimsy I let open my imagination, and started looking up other possible beings. I was wary about falling in with rough customers, but for some reason I reached out to Merlin; and got a response which was in feeling halfway between 'the best of luck in your search' and 'you'll need it acting like this' with a message that it was a bit reckless to just go chatting up what ever answers to a name in the imagination; after all you don't know who your really talking to. The wind blew and I thought about Hu, then it blew really hard and I got shy, so I reached out to Olwen, or vise versa. She felt to be colored like rabbit brush just before the spring leaves emerge, all hay yellow and pastel green; an extremely pleasant presence, which felt generally supportive and sympathetic; but was also not the being I was looking for. The power of both above described interactions was surprising to say the least.
My final question is, is it a good idea to reach out for spiritual assistance, in theory an interlocutor, in my meditation efforts, and if so should I simply use the prayer mentioned elsewhere "I believe and hope there is a god or goddess who is willing to work with me and I would like to make contact with that deity" or would something more formal like meeting in an ovate temple (like meeting someone meet via the internet is often best done in a public or safe environemnt) be more appropriate; or would such explorations be better put off until I have on my own perseverance made it a ways further along the path?
Ray Wharton

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Don't go back to the beginning when you stop practicing for a while. That's one of the Watcher's classic tricks. Always, always, always pick up where you left off. You can always go back over it again later when you've finished. Pick up where you left off and just keep on plodding away. How long it will take you is anyone's guess, as it depends on how much effort you put into it.

With regard to reaching out for spiritual assistance, you seem to have gotten good results with that; why not keep doing the same thing?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Classic trick indeed! Nasty trick too, it is so much harder to meditate on things where one has already gotten the fruit one is tall enough to reach. Thank you for this 'magic monday' thing you do, it is an extraordinary resource; like I imagined the ye olde correspondences courses, except adapted to a digital period.

I was more or less intending on continuing along the same track, its just that the response was so potent that I figured it was smart to check in with a more experienced. Figure I will try more of the same, maybe extend and invite to a temple if a promising lead comes up, judge the tree by the fruit, and trust the lesser ritual of the pentagram as a adequate basic safety tool if any rascals come by. Though, come to think of it, spirits (or the like) of widely varying degrees of helpfulness have been a part of my life for a long time, extending back to, through, and behind my skeptical days.

comments.title: Not drawn to scale or proportion...
comments.content: The instructions for the LBRP in the Celtic Golden dawn say to imagine yourself growing until your head is in the stars, the sun is at your solar plexus, and you're standing on the earth. That's impossible according to actual scale, obviously, so how large would you suggest visualizing the sun and earth as I'm doing the ritual?

replies.title: Re: Not drawn to scale or proportion...
replies.content: A follow up question to this: When you're doing the Pentagram rituals and you're imagining this, or anything, should your eyes be open or closed?

replies.title: Re: Not drawn to scale or proportion...
replies.content: Try both and see which works best for you. It differs from person to person.

replies.title: Re: Not drawn to scale or proportion...
replies.content: Each of them should be between 8 and 12 inches across. No, they're not to scale.

comments.title: Self talk
comments.content:
I've been following daily discursive meditation, SOP, and oracle readings with very satisfactory results. I haven't been able to go much further because, well, you really weren't kidding when you said that the universe has plenty of forces just waiting to conspire to prevent this kind of work!

My question is in regards to the bad habit of talking out loud to myself. Is that something that is naturally addressed in due course following say the Celtic Golden Dawn, or are there particular traditions or techniques for (ideally) eliminating it?

Curmudgeonly grumbling runs in my family, but mine has morphed in to almost spontaneous 5 second long possessions where I'll have an unprovoked verbal outburst, just enough to put my foot firmly in my mouth for the audience I swear some entity conjures out of nowhere for my humiliation. Comedy gold if it wasn't happening to me!

replies.title: Re: Self talk
replies.content: Figure out what benefit or pleasure you gain from doing that, and you're halfway there...

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Sir Greer,

1) Why did Golden Dawn put Le Fou before The Magician, making The Magician, Beth instead of Aleph? Doesn't this mess up the correspondences of the Tarot's number and the Hebrew letter's numerical value?

2) How often should one do a momentary Tarot reading? As in, how long should one meditate upon the reading? For example: Doing a reading once a week and meditating upon it throughout a given time VS doing a reading every day. Is it possible to do a reading too much to the point where they lose significance (i.e. not liking your reading and doing one until you get one you like). I'm not sure how long to let a reading sit before I do a new one.

3) AE Waite is quite confusing in his elaborations on the Tarot in his Holy Kabbalah [book]. He says: "Now there is one card which bears no number and is therefore allocated according to the discretion of the interpreter. It has been allocated in all cases wrongly, by the uninstructed because they had nothing but their private judgment to guide them, and by those who knew better because they desired to mislead. I may go further and say that the true nature of Tarot symbolism is perhaps a secret in the hands of a very few persons, and outside that circle
operators and writers may combine the cards as they like and attribute them as they like, but they will never find the right way" (pg 482, 483, http://www.hermetics.org/pdf/waite/A.E_Waite__The_Doctrine_and_Literature_of_the_Kabalah.pdf)

He also goes on to say they are not the key to the Kabbalah as Papus might have mislead people into thinking. He says there is no historical evidence, though he does admit a personal belief that there is some symbolic connection.

My question is: Was Waite a GD member? Where does the Fool belong? What is this correct method of Tarot that lies in the hands of only a few? Is it the one taught in GD? Also, why did he switch 8 and 11, Justice and Strength?

4) When one says YHVH, do they say Yod-He-Vau-He or Yahweh/Jehova? For instance, in the Pentagram ritual, you say ADONAI, the full word pronounced, not split up into its letters.

5) Yesod is likened to the Ether. Is the Astral Light the same thing? Or is Astral Light everything and ether/Yesod fall under its category? What's the difference between the two?

6) When performing active imagination of any kind, when you visualize yourself doing something, what POV is the correct/best if any? I tend to find myself doing 3rd person as 1st person is more difficult, but is it something that I should be working on?

Thank you very much for taking the time to read this; this is my first Magic Monday and I have a lot to catch up for!

Blessings!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Hmm. That's quite a laundry list -- and by the way, I'm not Sir Anything, unless you're addressing me in certain Masonic contexts.

Questions 1, 3, and 5 show me that you need to do considerably more reading in the basic history and theory of magic. These are things you can easily find out in books on the history of modern magic, and as a student of occultism, you need to get into the habit of learning things for yourself rather than asking me. I'd encourage you to start with Israel Regardie's The Tree of Life and Ellic Howe's The Magicians of the Golden Dawn, and go from there.

Questions 2 and 6 are a matter of what works best for you personally. Try the various options and pay attention to what gives you the best results.

Question 4 depends on who you ask. My books on the subject specify a particular pronunciation; other books specify different pronunciations. Use the one given by the book you're using as a text for practice.

That latter's the crucial point, of course. It doesn't matter what other people do or don't do -- if you're going to practice a system of occultism, choose a basic textbook and follow it to the letter. Once you've finished it -- as in, practiced everything in the book -- you'll then have the necessary background to make such questions make sense.

comments.title: Regardie’s “Art of True Healing”
comments.content:
For many weeks I’ve been doing a spell from Israel Regardie’s “The Art of True Healing.” It’s the one that involves planetary magic in association with the circulation of the light. My desired objective is success in a business known for not being particularly easy to succeed in.

The problem is I’m getting no mojo - no sense of anything happening, and no desired events. Before, when I started with another planet, I got results: weird dreams, an eye injury and electrocution. But at any rate something was happening. Now my practice is safer and more sound, but in terms of results I might as well be sitting around picking my nose for all the action I’m getting.

So what do I need to change? Ought I to alter or simplify the content of my request? Should I ask the god, or engage in some other form of divination? Drop this practice and change to another approach altogether?

A bit of background: in this particular business I have been making efforts on the material plane for many, many years. My experience has been that however hard or persistently I may try, I get stomped. I get flattened. I get my booty kicked. I get squashed into the waffle of somebody’s boot. Yet when people see my work, it is obvious to them that this is my true calling. What gives? And if I am somehow getting in my own way, how can I stop that and do something productive instead?

Kevin

replies.title: Re: Regardie’s “Art of True Healing”
replies.content:
Have you consulted a competent astrologer and asked what your chart has to say about your chances of succeeding in that business?

One of the hard lessons of occultism is that magic isn't omnipotent. You can want something very, very badly, and everyone around you may tell you that you ought to have it, and for a variety of reasons it may be out of your reach. In my experience, if sustained magical practice produces no result, you may need to explore the possibility that you're not going to get what you want, and redirect your magical efforts to something else.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
What I'm about to say feels a little stupid, since I thought I had "gotten" this but every once in a while it becomes clear to me that I haven't.

Two things that occasionally bother me about Dion Fortune's definition of magic as "causing change in consciousness in accordance of will" are (a) it can make a lot of magic seem, well, unmagical, and (b) it seems hard to reconcile it with older conceptions of magic, such as tapping into astrological currents.

As an example of (a), your discussion of curses above sounds like simple psychology rather than magic: it just makes someone notice a trap they're already in, so that they become hyper-conscious of it, leading them to spring it and start thrashing about, increasing the damage it does.

So I guess my question is: given Fortune's definition, is there actually a difference between magic and psychology? Or are the more "magical" forms of magic simply applied psychology through a different medium (e.g. the astral light) than usual? (As an analogy, air can't carry a sound wave loud enough to kill you, but water can; SONAR pings are lethal to anything swimming nearby. So perhaps there are spells you can cast without the astral light, and other spells that require it as a, ahem, component.)

Perhaps I'm having trouble grasping this because of my continuing fondness for formal causation as an explanation of magic, and seeing free will as an essentially magical phenomenon. (You may remember a long, rambling comment from me a long time ago on WoG to that effect.) "Active formal causation" — using a Form/morphic field/what have you to influence the outcome of events — strikes me as the notion that reconciles the various conceptions of magic and differentiate them all from more mundane types of processes.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Dion Fortune argued that all of magic can be summed up in two things; autosuggestion and the astral light. I think she was a little too narrow in her definitions, but both are heavily involved in every magical act. What differentiates magic from psychology is, first, magic has been around a lot longer and includes a lot of tricks psychologists haven't learned yet, and second, magic works with the astral light as the medium through which consciousness flows from mind to mind.

Second, concerning a comment from someone else last week: chalk up another person who's been feeling a staticky sensation in the throat and to a lesser extent heart regions. I'm not sure that's the same phenomenon as the commenter last week, but it's similar enough to be worth noting. If it is the same thing, however, it's affecting certain regions of the country for longer than others, since I've been feeling it since sometime in 2013 or 2014. Mind you, if it was going to affect different areas at different rates I would not be surprised if my part of America was one of the first areas affected; the land here is quite nice, but there's something on top of it that's another matter entirely and it's seeped into the culture here.

I haven't noticed any staticky sensation in my gut, but it's possible I just haven't noticed it... and 2013/2014 is roughly when my digestion started going haywire so there is that.

More concerningly... well, I'm really hoping that the tendency for The Battle Hymn of the Republic and to a lesser extent Dixie to run through my head when I hear static noise since Charlottesville is just my head and broadly reflective of the state of the astral these days. (On really bad days it sounds like the Battle Hymn is being sung by a children's choir.)

comments.title: Another SOP beginner
comments.content: Howdy!  I've also been working through the SOP ritual.  I'm new, and so I'm only up to a few weeks of invoking and banishing water and air, and waiting to move on to the other elements.  Currently, when doing the elemental cross, I simply invoke the elements themselves, without naming a deity.  After reading the experiences of others, I began calling out to a deity, and after a few attempts, had visions of a green skinned, and green haired female.  She presented me with a vision of a waterfall, a pine tree, and a mountain, though she hasn't told me her name. I'd like to balance my personal experience with historical knowledge, but the mythology texts I have and some cursory googling haven't led me to anything promising. I was wondering if these images resonate with a deity that folks might be familiar with and if there are any red flags in my practice that might make me susceptible to leading myself astray or being led astray.  Many thanks!

replies.title: Re: Another SOP beginner
replies.content: Don't worry about history yet. Treat your encounters with the goddess the way you would treat your encounters with a respected and beloved human being. Ask her for her name, and anything else she's willing to tell you about herself, and see where the relationship goes.

replies.title: Re: Medication and magic

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Thanks for doing this, JMG. I always find reading through the Q&A's fascinating.

So my question is prompted by your occasional comment about how Christianity spread through Europe in part because Christian priests were perceived as being great at magic... I spent much of my twenties in Christian high liturgical traditions, and the more I think about it the more I'm intrigued... In the Eastern Orthodox liturgy, the official start of the liturgy is preceded by several rites, one of which is the priest circling around the church and censing the entire congregation. This is probably asking for rampant speculation, but to what extent do you think this accomplishing a similar effect to the sphere of protection or a banishing ritual?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's not speculation at all. Charles Leadbeater, a Theosophist who got deep into Christian ritual, describes the censing and several other details of the opening of the Mass in exactly those terms in his book The Science of the Sacraments. Leadbeater had his personal problems, no question, but he was a capable clairvoyant and I see no reason to argue with his descriptions.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
During the earliest centuries of the Christian era, while it had not yet become an established religion of any empire or kingdom, one of the more common Pagan accusations against Christians was that churches were nothing other than regular assemblies of evil magicians. This was a serious accusation, and could lead to capital charges and execution; during those centuries "evil magician" was as damning a label as "communist" in the US during the McCarthy era.

Nor was it an unreasonable accusation. Here, by way of example, are several ways in which early Christian pratices actually did fit the popular (and legal) Roman conception of what "evil magicians" did. This list could be made much longer.

(1) Christians came together in secret meetings at night, or at dawn or dusk.

(2) They performed rituals together, such as the Eucharist, and they performed these rituals out of public view, excluding outsiders.

(3) Their rituals made use of parts of the bodies of executed criminals (relics of the martyrs and saints). (Even now, a very small piece of such a relic, usually a fragment of human bone, is part of a properly made and consecrated altar in Catholic and Orthodox traditions.)

(4) They often came together for their meetings in places where the dead were buried, that is, in graveyards and catacombs, for easier access to the remains of the dead. (These are, of course, the places where they could approach the tombs and relics of the martyrs and saints.)

(5) They ate and drank what they claimed were the flesh and blood of at least one executed criminal (Jesus). Even if they claimed to do this only in the form of symbols, still it was the thought that counted with the Roman public.

(6) Each of them claimed to have, or hoped to have, an spirit to accompany him or her throughout life and after death (the Holy Spirit).

(7) Each of them had undergone a ritual initiation symbolizing his or her own death (Baptism), which resembled the ritual deaths involved in some forms of magical initiation.

(8) In their secret rituals, they made use of special robes, altar tools, incense, and so forth, and by these means they called down a spirit (the Holy Spirit), and interacted with spirits of the dead (the communion of the Saints).

And so forth ...

So the accusation of "magician" brought against the early Christians has considerable substance to it. This posed an enormous PR problem for the emergent official Christian Churches.

Early Christian theologians dealt with this problem by elaborating complex theories to distinguish between "magic" and "miracle," or "evil" and "good" ritual power. Mostly these theories turn on questions of just Who empowers a given ritual (God the Creator of All Things versus lesser, evil spirits).

I wasn't raised Christian, so my knowledge of traditional Christian ritual is an outside observer's knowledge, though a fairly detailed one; I am an academic Medievalist, and this knowledge is part of my "stock in trade." Also, the rituals I know best are Eastern Orthodox ones, not Roman Catholic ones. But even as an academic outsider, I could perceive real power and presence when these rituals are enacted. They are far more than a game -- much less a sham -- played with ancient symbols and texts. I don't doubt that the Roman magistrates also were aware of the power of these rituals, back in the first centuries of the Christian era, and that the early Pagan attacks on Christians as magicians seemed quite reasonable to their well-informed readers who were not themselves Christians.

-- Robert Mathiesen

comments.title: Can spirits leave physical traces?
comments.content:
I’ve just had my third odd, perhaps supernatural, experience in less than a year. Quite a lot of weirdness for a guy that can’t think of more than a handful of unexplained happenings in his entire 50-year existence. For a couple months I’ve had a recurring dream or dream like experience in bed at different times of the night and in the early predawn morning hours. I sleep next to a wall with window that is always covered by an opaque shade outside the window is a dense forest under a couple feet of snow. The forest comes to within six feet of the window. All this is factual and uncontroversial. In my experience a very tiny perfect deer, more like the platonic form of a deer than a real deer, walks to the window which I can see through in my dream state and begins talking to me. The message is largely meaningless to me although it always makes me feel good or happy even though I don’t understand it. Once I’m fully awake in the morning I tell my wife that the deer was back and she laughs at me. This happened numerous times. Then my wife and I walked around the house, hard to do in 2 feet of snow, to clear a path to a generator and lo and behold there are tiny deer tracks right up to the corner of the house nearest where I sleep. Not to the window but to the corner right where I sleep. These tracks appear a couple times always to the same corner and retrace themselves perfectly back into the woods from which they came.

Assuming we aren’t just crazy or making spurious correlations, my question is this. Even in living animist world of many beings and nature spirits how would this be possible? The planes don’t touch each other so presumably a spiritual being wouldn’t be a deer? Or is this how a spiritual being, an “other bodied” nature spirit, might appear in the physical world as a pleasant but unintelligible talking deer? Can spirits leave physical traces?
